content.text: MTTD, MTTA, and MTTR Tracking for Claude Incidents

Mean Time to Detect (MTTD), Mean Time to Acknowledge (MTTA), and Mean Time to Resolve (MTTR) are the three primary operational health metrics for an incident management organisation. Together they measure how quickly a failure becomes visible, how quickly the team engages, and how quickly the system is restored to normal. For an AI service like Claude, each metric has specific nuances that differ from traditional web service incident management.

These are lagging indicators — they measure past performance, not future risk. They are most useful as trend signals: is detection getting faster or slower over quarters? Is MTTR correlated with severity as expected, or is there an unusual pattern? The numbers themselves matter less than what they reveal about systemic gaps.

attrs.alt: Incident time metrics on a single timeline: T0 (failure starts), T1 (alert fires), T2 (on-call acknowledges), T3 (mitigation deployed), T4 (resolved). MTTD spans T0-T1, MTTA spans T1-T2, MTTM spans T0-T3, MTTR spans T0-T4.

content.text: MTTD is the average time from when a failure begins to when the monitoring system detects it and fires an alert. For Claude API incidents, the start-of-failure timestamp is the time of the first anomalous event in the metrics — the first error rate spike, the first latency outlier, or the first safety classifier anomaly. Not the time a customer reported it.
Time from failure start to first PagerDuty alert
Time from failure start to first human awareness (may be customer report)
Used when alert fires late or not at all
Time from failure start to first customer support ticket
Lagging indicator of alert coverage gaps

If Human MTTD is significantly shorter than Alert MTTD for the same incident, a customer noticed the problem before the monitoring system did. This is a detection gap and requires a postmortem action item.

MTTA is the average time from when an alert fires to when the on-call engineer acknowledges it in PagerDuty. MTTA measures the availability and responsiveness of the on-call rotation — it is not a measure of investigation quality.
Page IC and escalation contact simultaneously at 5 minutes
Escalate to secondary on-call at 10 minutes
No automatic escalation; review if missed

MTTM measures the time from failure start to when the impact is mitigated — the root cause may not be fixed, but customer-visible symptoms are resolved or substantially reduced. For Claude API incidents, mitigation typically means rolling back a change, rerouting traffic, scaling a cluster, or disabling a problematic feature flag.

MTTM is a more actionable metric than MTTR for AI incidents because resolution (full root cause fix) may take days if it requires a model retraining, but mitigation can often happen in minutes. Tracking them separately shows whether the team is good at stopping the bleeding even if the wound takes longer to heal.

MTTR is the total incident duration: time from failure start to when the incident is marked RESOLVED on the status page. It is the sum of MTTD + MTTA + time-to-investigate + MTTM + stabilisation window. For Claude API, the stabilisation window is typically 15–30 minutes after mitigation — the time to confirm error rates have returned to baseline and are not re-spiking.

The accuracy of all four metrics depends on collecting precise timestamps from authoritative sources. Manual timestamp recording — an IC writing 'T+15 minutes: deployed fix' in Slack — is unreliable. The following sources are authoritative for each timestamp.
First anomaly in Grafana time series (error rate, latency P95, GPU memory)
First customer support ticket if monitoring gap
Slack bot message if PD not used
Slack reaction timestamp on alert message
Deployment system (GitHub Actions, Argo CD) deployment completion time
Individual incident metrics are less meaningful than the distribution across many incidents. Mean values are influenced by outliers; P50 and P90 give a more honest picture of typical performance.

These are P50 (median) targets — half of incidents should beat them. P90 targets are typically 2–3x the P50. An incident that significantly misses the P90 target triggers a specific postmortem section on the outlier cause.

For Claude API incidents where the failure is in model behaviour — unexpected refusals, quality regressions, safety filter false positives — MTTR is structurally longer than for infrastructure incidents. Fixing a model quality issue may require a new model release, retraining, or a safety evaluation cycle that takes days. In these cases, MTTR is not a useful operational metric on its own.

For AI quality incidents, the team tracks MTTM (time to mitigate the symptom via a feature flag, model version rollback, or traffic rerouting) separately from MTTR (time to full resolution via model fix). Reporting both prevents the MTTR for AI quality incidents from making the infrastructure metrics look worse than they are.
Full resolution is typically achievable in hours
Full resolution requires model cycle; mitigation is actionable in minutes
Safety evaluation takes time; quarantining the affected classifier is fast
Typically recoverable; MTTR is the right signal

Some Claude API failures are silent: the API returns 200 but the quality is degraded. These incidents have a MTTD that reflects when a quality monitoring signal — not a standard error rate alert — fires. Quality monitoring for Claude API includes:
Output length distribution: P5 response length dropping significantly indicates truncation or early stopping
Refusal rate: percentage of requests where stop_reason is 'end_turn' with no content
Tool call success rate: percentage of tool-use responses with valid JSON structure
Embedding distribution drift: for applications using embeddings, cosine similarity drift between daily samples

MTTD, MTTA, MTTM, and MTTR are reported monthly at the team level and quarterly in the reliability review. Trend direction matters more than absolute values — a team with a P0 MTTR of 45 minutes that is improving is in better health than a team with a 20-minute MTTR that is degrading.

## Incident Metrics Report — {month} {year}

### P0/P1 Incidents ({count} total)
| Metric | P50 | P90 | vs. Prior Month | Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MTTD | {mttd_p50} min | {mttd_p90} min | {delta} | < 5 min |
| MTTA | {mtta_p50} min | {mtta_p90} min | {delta} | < 5 min |
| MTTM | {mttm_p50} min | {mttm_p90} min | {delta} | < 30 min |
| MTTR | {mttr_p50} min | {mttr_p90} min | {delta} | < 60 min |

### Outliers (P90 exceeded)
- INC-{id}: MTTR {value} min — cause: {cause}

### Trend Flags
- MTTD trending UP (+{pct}% vs. last quarter) — monitoring coverage review scheduled
Using the customer report time as T0 instead of the first monitoring signal — this inflates MTTD and understates detection quality
Using the incident declaration time (IC creates the channel) as T0 instead of the failure start time — gives false MTTR improvement
Mixing P0 and P3 incidents in the same MTTR average — P3 incidents are expected to take longer and dilute the P0 signal
Not tracking MTTM separately for AI quality incidents — makes infrastructure MTTR look artificially high
Computing mean without outlier analysis — a single 12-hour incident dominates the mean and hides 9 well-handled incidents
Reporting MTTA from Slack messages instead of PagerDuty events — Slack timestamps are less reliable and not available for review

MTTD is a lagging metric — it measures detection speed for incidents that were detected. It says nothing about the incidents that went undetected until a customer complained. Alert coverage analysis answers the forward-looking question: for which known failure modes do we not have an alert?
An alert coverage matrix maps each known failure mode from the postmortem library to a monitoring signal. Gaps are failure modes with no corresponding alert.
Safety classifier refusal rate > 5% above baseline

Every gap in the coverage matrix is a candidate for a Detection action item. When prioritising: estimate the blast radius if the failure mode were to occur without detection — gaps with P0 blast radius are P1-band action items.

Periodically injecting a known failure mode in a non-production environment and measuring whether the alert fires — and how quickly — tests the reliability of the alert itself. An alert that is configured but never tested may be silently broken.

from datetime import datetime, timezone

def run_synthetic_detection_test(
    failure_mode: str,
    inject_fn,        # callable that triggers the failure in staging
    expected_alert_name: str,
    pagerduty_client,
    timeout_minutes: int = 10,
) -> dict:
    start = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    inject_fn()
    deadline = start.timestamp() + timeout_minutes * 60
    while datetime.now(timezone.utc).timestamp() < deadline:
        alerts = pagerduty_client.list_alerts(
            since=start.isoformat(),
            service_key="claude-staging",
        )
        matches = [a for a in alerts if expected_alert_name in a["title"]]
        if matches:
            detected_at = datetime.fromisoformat(matches[0]["created_at"])
            mttd_seconds = (detected_at - start).total_seconds()
            return {
                "failure_mode": failure_mode,
                "detected": True,
                "mttd_seconds": mttd_seconds,
                "alert": matches[0]["title"],
            }
    return {
        "failure_mode": failure_mode,
        "detected": False,
        "mttd_seconds": None,
        "alert": None,
        "finding": f"Alert '{expected_alert_name}' did not fire within {timeout_minutes} min",
    }

MTTA is partially a measure of alert routing quality and on-call schedule health, not just individual responsiveness. An on-call engineer cannot acknowledge an alert they did not receive, and escalation policies must account for time zones, holidays, and schedule gaps.
Alert routed to a stale phone number in PagerDuty — the on-call engineer rotated but the schedule was not updated
Alert sent to a Slack channel that the on-call engineer does not have notifications enabled for
On-call engineer is in a meeting or on a flight — no phone notification received
Alert escalation policy has a gap: secondary on-call not configured, so escalation goes to a team channel instead of a person
Alert suppressed by a maintenance window that was not removed after the maintenance period ended
PagerDuty escalation log shows wrong first-notified user
Weekly schedule review; bot that pings the current on-call to confirm
PagerDuty setup checklist for new on-call engineers
Alert created but no page fired; maintenance window log shows overlap
Expiry alerts on maintenance windows; 24h maximum duration policy
Alert went to team channel, not a person
Audit escalation policy; ensure all P0 policies end with a named individual

MTTD, MTTA, MTTM, and MTTR are four distinct metrics measuring different phases of an incident lifecycle. Each has an authoritative timestamp source, a severity-specific target, and specific nuances for Claude AI incidents. MTTM and MTTR are tracked separately for AI quality incidents because full model resolution is structurally slower than infrastructure mitigation. P50 and P90 percentiles reveal the distribution better than mean values. Trend direction over quarters is the primary signal — the absolute numbers are secondary.
T0 is always the first anomaly in monitoring, not the alert or the customer report
MTTA targets: P0 < 5 min, P1 < 10 min, P2 < 30 min — measured from PagerDuty alert to ack
MTTM and MTTR are tracked separately for AI quality incidents
Track P50 and P90, not just mean — outliers dominate means
Monthly trend reports and quarterly aggregates are the minimum reporting cadence
Silent failure detection requires quality-specific metrics: refusal rate, output length, tool call success rate
